I'm not a fan of roll your own. Looking at the code __generic_file_aio_write is suppose to be lockless. The function generic_file_aio_write takes the i_mutex lock. Are you sure SLES is that broken?

Patch Set 3:

Patch Set 3: Code-Review-1

Lete just change it to generic_file_aio_write().

Patch Set 3:

James,

Take a look at https://jira.hpdd.intel.com/browse/LU-1669 and the patches for it.

We switch away from generic_file_aio_write for a very good reason.

Patch Set 3:

Also, Ben, I about this - We also need to fix the _iter code to use the lockless version, if we want to keep the LU-1669 performance improvements in newer kernels.

Patch Set 1:

Build Started https://build.hpdd.intel.com/job/lustre-reviews/38572/

Patch Set 3:

Well that is sucky. Ben work brings back the mutex as well.

Patch Set 3: Code-Review-1

(3 comments)

I'm not wholly against making a copy of the common kernel code, since this is time limited (it will disappear with 3.16+ kernels that use the ->write_iter() interface.

However, much more important in the long term is fixing the ->write_iter() code to avoid taking i_mutex for all writes.  That seems relatively straight forward in itself, but there may be some possibility to consolidate code between all of these different versions.

Patch Set 1: Verified-1

Build Failed 
 
https://build.hpdd.intel.com/job/lustre-reviews/38572/ : FAILURE

Patch Set 3:

(1 comment)

I do want to point out the reason for this new lustre_**() function is to take the lock. What Ben produces still takes the lock mind over over a small range of code but it will still hurt performance.

I took a look at the xfs code and I noticed that file_remove_suid() only needs to be called when IS_SEC(inode) is false. We could instead have something like

if (!IS_SEC(inode))
     generic_file_aio_write(...)
else
     __generic_file_aio_write(...)

This way the pain is only for those files with suid is set which is rare. The bonus unlike the posted patch here is that we avoid taking the mutex expect for that rare case.

Yes I need to devote cycles to fixing the newer kernel issues. I will do that under the JIRA ticket opened for that issue.

If you want to use the CRAY ticket you need to make it public. I will need to push this patch upstream and having a ticket that can't be viewed by the public is a no-no.

Patch Set 3:

James,

Hm, that's a really sound idea, and it *looks* like that check is done without the i_mutex taken.

Maybe that could be applied to http://review.whamcloud.com/#/c/19840/ as well, as the same problem applies there?  (I just reviewed over there...)

However, there's probably a race where someone can set the suid flag while we're calling __generic_file_aio_write...  Is that a concern?  I'll have to think about that some more.

Patch Set 3:

Mm, further reflection suggests that that's a problem: It would let people trigger the suid modification code from __generic_file_aio_write, and if running that's a problem, then...  Ugh.

Patch Set 9:

Just to repeat my question from the previous patch - is NOSEC being set on all non-SUID inodes by the VFS or is this something we need to do in the llite code?  Otherwise this patch has the effect of getting i_mutex all the time. 

Also, it is worthwhile to check if NOSEC needs to be set/cleared during inode revalidate (ll_setattr() and ll_update_inode())?

Patch Set 9:

It looks like it's getting the lock for each write.  I'll revise the patch.

parents.subject: Revert "LU-5050 libcfs: default CPT matches NUMA topology"

commit.subject: LU-8162 llite: lock i_mutex when suid bit changes
commit.message:
LU-8162 llite: lock i_mutex when suid bit changes

In SLES12 __generic_file_aio_write assumes i_mutex is locked.
In Lustre it is not, as protections are handled elsewhere.
This causes a stack dump the first time file_remove_suid() is called,
and we'd rather not see that.

Signed-off-by: Ben Evans <bevans@cray.com>
Change-Id: If3770913a670cb17219ff0a262dbb6ba2be75dec
